skill.name: flow-nexus-swarm
skill.description: Guides cloud AI swarm orchestration, workflow automation, agent assignment, monitoring, and Flow Nexus setup.
skill.summary: Coordinate Flow Nexus cloud swarms and event-driven workflows for multi-agent automation.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-crypto and external-command patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, prose, and example MCP calls. The remaining risk is medium because the skill directs users to run npm/npx commands, add an MCP server, and use an external cloud orchestration service. No prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: External Cloud Orchestration Requires Data Disclosure Review
medium_findings.description: The skill requires a Flow Nexus account and describes cloud-based swarm orchestration. Workflow inputs, task descriptions, agent names, and execution metadata may be sent to an external service, which is expected for this skill but should be disclosed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly requires an active Flow Nexus account and repeatedly describes cloud-based orchestration. I found no evidence of covert exfiltration, but external processing is central to the skill.

medium_findings.title: User-Run Package Installation and MCP Server Commands
medium_findings.description: The setup section instructs users to install Flow Nexus, run npx commands, log in, and register an MCP server. These are legitimate setup steps, but they execute third-party packages and create supply-chain and local command execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible setup instructions rather than hidden execution. Risk depends on the user choosing to run them and on the integrity of the referenced npm packages.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static external-command detections point to JavaScript and Bash examples in Markdown, not Ruby or shell backtick execution inside an executable script. The examples describe MCP calls and setup commands for the documented platform.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows Markdown fences and documented command snippets. There is no standalone script file or dynamic command construction in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detections Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto lines contain ordinary prose, field names, or workflow examples, not cryptographic algorithms, hash functions, encryption APIs, or security-sensitive crypto configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations do not show DES, MD5, SHA-1, or similar crypto usage. The scanner appears to have matched substrings such as description, destroy, or infrastructure terms.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Expected
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the Flow Nexus platform, documentation, and issue tracker. They are normal support links, not hidden callbacks or data-transfer endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a Support and Resources section and are visible to users. No request code, credential handling, or background network behavior is present in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is a Command Example
low_findings.description: The reported system-reconnaissance line is part of a documented claude-flow hook command. It does not inspect host details or collect system inventory in this file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a Bash example for exporting task metrics. I found no evidence of uname, env dumping, filesystem inventory, or similar reconnaissance behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party CLI Execution Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes npm, npx, and claude mcp setup commands that execute external packages or register a local MCP server. This pattern is legitimate for setup but should be visible in marketplace risk warnings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and user-facing. They are not hidden automation, but they rely on external package sources and local command execution.

content.value_statement: Multi-agent projects are hard to plan, assign, monitor, and scale across workflows. This skill provides Flow Nexus patterns for cloud swarms, event-driven automation, queue monitoring, and reusable templates.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize Flow Nexus swarms with hierarchical, mesh, ring, or star topologies.
Shows how to spawn specialized agents for research, coding, analysis, optimization, and coordination.
Provides workflow creation patterns with dependencies, triggers, priorities, retries, and async execution.
Documents monitoring commands for swarm status, workflow status, queues, audit trails, and execution streams.
Includes reusable patterns for full-stack development, research, CI/CD, ETL, and multi-swarm coordination.
Covers Flow Nexus authentication, CLI installation, and Claude Code MCP server setup.
limitations: Requires a Flow Nexus MCP server and an active Flow Nexus account.
Does not implement the Flow Nexus service or validate account permissions.
Examples are instructional and depend on installed CLI tools and available MCP functions.
Cloud workflows may process project metadata outside the local environment.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Delivery
use_cases.description: Plan a swarm topology, assign specialist agents, and monitor progress for complex software projects.

use_cases.title: Automate Research Workflows
use_cases.description: Use mesh swarms and parallel task orchestration to gather, analyze, and summarize information.

use_cases.title: Design Cloud Automation Pipelines
use_cases.description: Create event-driven CI/CD, ETL, and incident workflows with retries, queues, and audit trails.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Swarm
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a Flow Nexus swarm topology for a small project with three agents. Explain the agent roles and first commands.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Workflow Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Flow Nexus workflow for testing, building, and deploying my app. Include dependencies, triggers, retries, and monitoring steps.

prompt_templates.title: Map Agents to Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Break this project into agent tasks and recommend a Flow Nexus swarm strategy, agent types, priorities, and assignment rules.

prompt_templates.title: Operate a Multi-Swarm Program
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a multi-swarm Flow Nexus program for research, implementation, QA, and deployment. Include lifecycle cleanup and audit requirements.

output_examples.input: Plan a swarm for a full-stack project with backend, frontend, database, and QA work.
output: Recommended topology: hierarchical with a coordinator and four specialist agents.
Suggested workflow: requirements, database design, backend, frontend, integration, and QA.
Monitoring plan: check swarm status, workflow status, queue health, and audit trail after each phase.

output_examples.input: Create an async ETL workflow with validation and retries.
output: Recommended workflow: extract, validate raw data, transform, enrich, load, and validate final output.
Execution mode: async queue processing with exponential backoff and three retries.
Operations plan: monitor queue depth, failed steps, and final validation results.

output_examples.input: Set up Flow Nexus for Claude Code.
output: Install the Flow Nexus CLI, register or log in, then add the Flow Nexus MCP server to Claude Code.
Confirm the MCP server is available before running swarm or workflow commands.
Review what project data may be sent to the cloud service before execution.

best_practices: Choose the simplest topology that matches the project dependency structure.
Use async execution and queue monitoring for long-running workflows.
Destroy swarms and review audit trails after each completed workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not send secrets or regulated data to cloud workflows without approval.
Do not scale agent counts before defining roles, dependencies, and success criteria.
Do not run setup commands from an untrusted package source.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan and operate Flow Nexus cloud swarms, workflows, queues, agent assignments, and monitoring steps.

faq.question: Does it require external services?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires the Flow Nexus MCP server and an active Flow Nexus account.

faq.question: Can it run workflows locally only?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on Flow Nexus cloud orchestration and suggests Claude Flow for local coordination.

faq.question: Is it suitable for CI/CD planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CI/CD workflow examples with testing, building, security checks, staging, and production deployment.

faq.question: What should users review before using it?
faq.answer: Users should review external package commands, MCP server setup, account permissions, and cloud data handling.

faq.question: Does it include runnable application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides MCP command examples and orchestration patterns, not a complete application implementation.
